%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
  Emulating Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) campaigns in controlled environments requires both behavioral knowledge of the adversary and a correctly configured \emph{System Under Test} (SUT), the target environment in which attack procedures execute. Despite the broad adoption of \mitre{} \attack{}, encoded in \stix{} bundles, the information necessary to derive concrete SUT requirements (software versions, exploitable vulnerabilities, infrastructure topology) is rarely expressed in structured form.
  
  We perform a systematic measurement of the \attack{} Enterprise, Mobile, and ICS datasets, with cross-corpus comparison to CAPEC and FiGHT, to quantify the availability, completeness, and specificity of SUT-relevant signals. We define a SUT-centric requirements model and metrics for coverage, version specificity, and deployment compatibility.
  
  Our results show that platform constraints are widespread but coarse, software references rarely include version identifiers (\softwarenoversionnocpepercentage\%), and vulnerability evidence is sparse and fragmented. Structured CTI alone does not support automated SUT generation. It does support coarse environment narrowing for most campaigns and deterministic backend planning for all techniques. In this dataset, SUT profile confusion drops to 0\% when groups carry at least two linked software items.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:introduction}

Controlled APT emulation requires two things: a description of \emph{what} an adversary does, and a concrete environment in which to do it. The first is increasingly well served by the \mitre{} \attack{} knowledge base, which now catalogues 835 Enterprise techniques across 187 documented groups and 52 campaigns, all encoded in machine-readable \stix{} bundles~\cite{strom2018mitre,DBLP:conf/uss/VirkudIR00024}. The second remains an open problem. Each emulation run requires a \emph{System Under Test (SUT)}: a configured environment whose OS family, installed software, exposed services, trust relationships, and vulnerable package versions must be specified before any technique can execute. The SUT choice determines which procedures are reachable, which privilege-escalation paths exist, and which CVEs are exploitable. Without a correct SUT, even a perfectly faithful procedure description produces either a silent no-op or an unreproducible result.

The central question this paper asks is simple: \emph{Can structured CTI provide enough information to derive SUT requirements automatically, and if not, where exactly does it fall short?} In adversary emulation workflows, CTI describes behavior, while the SUT defines the execution environment. Automation fails when environment constraints cannot be derived reliably from structured CTI representations. We measure that boundary by analyzing every SUT-relevant field across the \attack{} Enterprise, Mobile, and ICS corpora, with coverage comparison to CAPEC and FiGHT\@. Our findings are clear: platform constraints are broadly present but coarse; software references rarely include version specificity or CPE identifiers; and CVE linkages are sparse and split between structured and free-text encodings. Fully automated SUT generation from structured CTI alone is not feasible under current encoding practices. Structured fields do support coarse environment narrowing, backend-class planning, and evidence-filtered profile comparison.

The gap has practical consequences for the evaluation stack. Red team platforms such as \caldera{} provide execution logic but leave SUT instantiation to the analyst~\cite{applebaum2016caldera,redcanary2023art,orbinato2024laccolith,damodaran2025automated,wang2024sands}. APT detection systems are meaningfully evaluated only for techniques that can actually reach a target, and prior work repeatedly reports this dependence on realistic execution context (\cite{milajerdi2019holmes,han2020unicorn,DBLP:conf/uss/Dong0NS0LLX23,DBLP:conf/uss/YangXXLZ23,DBLP:conf/ccs/LvGQCZCJ24,DBLP:conf/sp/RehmanAH24}; see also~\cite{Li_2024,Cheng_2024,DBLP:conf/uss/JiaXN0ZW24,DBLP:conf/ccs/Aly0Y25,DBLP:conf/uss/Zhang0YK0KF25}). Attribution research also shows that robust actor-level conclusions remain difficult under TTP overlap, heterogeneous evidence quality, and incomplete reporting~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25,DBLP:conf/ccs/YuldoshkhujaevJ25}. Together, these observations increase dependence on richer contextual evidence, especially software and vulnerability context, which are precisely the signals most often missing from structured encoding. The same operational tension appears in recent analyses of ATT\&CK-oriented evaluation practice and deployment constraints in industry~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/VirkudIR00024,10.1145/3634737.3645012,DBLP:conf/ccs/DongLJLWHXCLGC23}.

A CTI standard that names malware without version information, or references a CVE only in prose, cannot drive automated SUT construction. We measure that gap.

\paragraph{Where structured CTI falls short}

CTI encodes \emph{what} was observed: which techniques appeared in a campaign, which malware was used, which groups were involved. It does not encode \emph{where} it ran. Translating a \stix{} bundle into a deployable environment requires knowing which OS version hosts a service, which package version carries a vulnerability, and which network topology enables lateral movement. None of these are structured fields in the current \attack{} encoding.

The \attack{} framework is a natural target for this measurement because it is widely adopted as structured CTI input for red-team platforms, detection evaluations, and threat-hunting programs~\cite{strom2018mitre,DBLP:conf/uss/VirkudIR00024}. Its role as a behavioral taxonomy, rather than an environment specification, is intentional~\cite{strom2018mitre,ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}. The gap appears when \attack{} is used as the sole structured input to emulation workflows that require environment detail. This paper measures that environment gap.

A companion study measured the procedural axis, i.e., whether structured CTI encodes sufficient detail to execute attack steps in the correct order. This paper isolates the environment axis: whether structured CTI encodes enough detail to build the target environment in which those steps can run. Separating these axes avoids conflating missing procedure details with missing SUT requirements. All measurements reported here are self-contained and computed directly from public STIX bundles; the companion citation is contextual.

\paragraph{Research questions}

\begin{description}
\item[RQ1 (Coverage):] How much SUT-relevant information is present in structured CTI objects and relationships, and how complete is it for deriving environment prerequisites such as platforms, software dependencies, and vulnerabilities?
\item[RQ2 (Deployability):] To what extent can software and vulnerability references be transformed into deployable SUT specifications for containers, VMs, or IaC, and what classes of requirements remain unmappable?
\item[RQ3 (Specificity):] How specific are SUT requirement profiles with respect to known intrusion sets and campaigns, and how reliably can they support susceptibility assessment without over-claiming attribution?
\end{description}

\paragraph{Contributions}

\begin{enumerate}
\item We define a SUT-centric measurement model that operationalizes CTI into environment prerequisites, covering platform, software, and vulnerability constraints.
\item A corpus-scale measurement quantifies the availability, sparsity, and ambiguity of SUT-relevant signals across \stix{} objects and relationships in the \mitre{} \attack{} Enterprise, Mobile, and ICS datasets, with cross-corpus coverage also measured for CAPEC and FiGHT\@.
\item A compatibility taxonomy classifies techniques by their minimum virtualization backend requirement (container-feasible, VM-required, or infrastructure-dependent).
\item We analyze SUT profile specificity across intrusion sets and discuss the limitations imposed by under-reporting and shared commodity behaviors~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}.
\item We release an audit-ready artifact with code, datasets, a validation packet, and one-command checks so that each quantitative claim is reproducible from the source bundles.
\end{enumerate}


% ===============================================================
\section{Background}\label{sec:background}

\subsection{The MITRE ATT\&CK Framework}

The \mitre{} \attack{} framework is a curated knowledge base documenting adversary tactics and techniques observed in real-world cyber operations. It organizes adversary behavior into a matrix of tactics (attacker objectives) and techniques (concrete methods), with sub-techniques capturing more specific procedural variants. The framework is divided into three domains: Enterprise (Windows, Linux, macOS, cloud, network infrastructure), Mobile, and Industrial Control Systems (ICS). The Enterprise domain is the most extensively documented, containing 835 techniques and sub-techniques across 14 tactics as of v18.1~\cite{strom2018mitre,DBLP:conf/uss/VirkudIR00024}.

\subsection{CTI Representation in STIX}

ATT\&CK content is distributed as \stix{} 2.x bundles~\cite{stixspec}. STIX defines a graph-based interchange format in which entities are typed STIX Domain Objects (SDOs) and connections are STIX Relationship Objects (SROs). The objects most relevant to SUT construction are \texttt{attack-pattern} (techniques), \texttt{campaign}, \texttt{intrusion-set}, \texttt{malware}, \texttt{tool}, and \texttt{relationship}. SROs encode semantic associations such as \emph{uses}, \emph{attributed-to}, and \emph{targets}, without specifying execution order, preconditions, or environment bindings.

The \stix{} schema does not define a structured procedure object. Ordering, environmental requirements, and parameter flows appear only in natural-language \texttt{description} fields and are not machine-readable. This design reflects the role of \attack{} as a behavioral taxonomy rather than a machine-actionable execution specification~\cite{strom2018mitre,ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}.

\subsection{Key Entities and SUT Relevance}

Table~\ref{tab:entities} summarizes the \attack{} entity types most relevant to SUT construction.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{ATT\&CK entity types and their SUT relevance.}\label{tab:entities}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{2.0cm} >{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{5.6cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Entity} & \textbf{SUT relevance} \\
\midrule
\texttt{attack-pattern} &
  Platform hints via \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}; system requirements in \texttt{x\_mitre\_system\_requirements} (rarely populated). \\
\texttt{malware} / \texttt{tool} &
  Implies OS dependency; version rarely specified; CPE mappings largely absent. \\
\texttt{campaign} &
  Scope operation to the time window and target set; links to techniques and intrusion set. \\
\texttt{intrusion-set} &
  Aggregates TTP profile; useful for SUT susceptibility profiling but not for single-campaign instantiation. \\
\texttt{vulnerability} &
  CVE-mapped objects are present in some bundles; they are rarely linked directly to campaigns. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Red team platforms such as \caldera{} assume a compatible SUT already exists and do not derive environment configurations from CTI~\cite{applebaum2016caldera}. SUT construction, therefore, remains a manual step that precedes emulation, motivating the core question of this paper: which parts are already supported by structured CTI, and which still require manual inference.


% ===============================================================
\section{Related Work}\label{sec:related}
% Citation governance note (quality scope):
% - Core evidence is top-4, arXiv, or standards.
% - The following non-top4 citations are intentionally retained because they
%   are either companion/internal baseline, foundational tooling, or
%   high-quality adjacent venues directly supporting specific claims:
%   10.1145/3634737.3645012, cheng2025ctinexus, shen2025pentestagent,
%   savat2021extractor, vermeer2022evolution, applebaum2016caldera,
%   orbinato2024laccolith, strom2018mitre, redcanary2023art,
%   furumoto2025ctisurvey,
%   ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured.

\myparagraph{CTI completeness and measurement}

Jin et al.\ show that CTI sharing provides limited operational value partly because shared feeds exhibit inconsistent field usage and low structural completeness~\cite{DBLP:conf/ndss/JinKLBKK24}. Vermeer et al.\ measure gaps in STIX-encoded sources~\cite{vermeer2022evolution}. Prior work surveys ATT\&CK's design, adoption, and known limitations~\cite{strom2018mitre,DBLP:conf/uss/VirkudIR00024}; it identifies sparse software versioning as an open problem but does not quantify it. Furumoto et al.\ provide a broad CTI survey with a measurement component over collected datasets and report dataset-construction and vendor-coverage sensitivities in published evaluations, but without a STIX-field-level SUT quantification model~\cite{furumoto2025ctisurvey}. Recent provenance-graph APT detection systems also show that operational coverage depends on what telemetry and ATT\&CK-linked behaviors are representable in the underlying datasets, and not just on model architecture~\cite{milajerdi2019holmes,han2020unicorn,DBLP:conf/uss/Dong0NS0LLX23,DBLP:conf/uss/YangXXLZ23,Li_2024,DBLP:conf/uss/JiaXN0ZW24,DBLP:conf/uss/Zhang0YK0KF25}. All of these works measure \emph{behavioral} completeness. None measures whether structured CTI contains enough information to instantiate the target environment required for emulation.

\myparagraph{CTI extraction pipelines}

Recent CTI extraction pipelines improve structured recovery of behavioral intelligence from reports, including TTP extraction and graph construction~\cite{savat2021extractor,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2412-10872,cheng2025ctinexus,DBLP:conf/uss/BuchelPLCZBE0GC25}. However, these lines of work still prioritize \emph{what} happened over \emph{where} execution should occur. As a result, they do not provide a measurement framework for assessing the quality of SUT derivation.

\myparagraph{Behavioral profiling and attribution specificity}

Practitioner-centered attribution research reports that analysts often prioritize understanding adversary TTPs, intent, and operational context over confidently naming a specific actor, due to ambiguity in available evidence and collaboration frictions~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}. Longitudinal analysis of public APT dossiers likewise highlights heterogeneity in report quality and evidence granularity across sources and periods~\cite{DBLP:conf/ccs/YuldoshkhujaevJ25}. Together, these studies indicate that analyst confidence is often bounded by evidence quality rather than by the absence of behavioral hypotheses~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25,DBLP:conf/ccs/YuldoshkhujaevJ25}. These findings motivate our focus on \emph{deployability}: whether software and CVE references carry enough structured information to instantiate a runnable target environment, independent of hard attribution claims.

\myparagraph{Adversary emulation frameworks}

Tools such as \caldera{}~\cite{applebaum2016caldera}, Atomic Red Team~\cite{redcanary2023art}, and planning-based systems such as AURORA~\cite{wang2024sands} assume a compatible SUT and provide execution logic. None automatically derives environment specifications from CTI. Laccolith~\cite{orbinato2024laccolith} and LLM-driven proposals~\cite{singer2025incalmo,deng2024pentestgpt,shen2025pentestagent} specify SUTs manually or rely on fixed lab topologies. Our work characterizes the extent to which CTI can contribute to this automation gap.

\myparagraph{Cyber range and testbed design}

Cyber range frameworks rely on reproducible environment definitions that specify OS images, service configurations, network topology, and vulnerable software. These specifications are authored manually and not derived from CTI~\cite{orbinato2024laccolith,damodaran2025automated}. Our findings quantify which CTI fields could, in principle, feed such specifications and which cannot, thereby bounding the scope of automated SUT derivation.

\myparagraph{The gap this paper fills}

The studies above collectively characterize CTI quality from a \emph{behavioral} perspective, including technique coverage, attribution specificity, and TTP extraction. To the best of our knowledge, prior studies have not explicitly measured whether structured CTI contains the environment information required to instantiate the target system used for emulation. The compatibility class of a technique (container, VM, or full infrastructure), the version specificity of referenced software, and the deployability of CVE constraints are not systematically measured in this problem setting. This paper operationalizes and measures these dimensions across five ATT\&CK-derived corpora, establishing a quantitative foundation for CTI standards that support automated SUT generation. Section~\ref{sec:definitions} turns this gap into an explicit measurement model and operational metrics.


% ===============================================================
\section{SUT Measurement Model}\label{sec:definitions}

We use a SUT-centric measurement model that operationalizes structured CTI objects into environment prerequisites. The model is intentionally minimal: it defines which constraints are required to instantiate an environment, and how those constraints are measured across datasets.

\subsection{SUT Constraint Types}

\myparagraph{Platform constraints}

A technique carries a platform constraint when \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms} lists a supported OS family, cloud provider, or device type. This is the coarsest SUT signal: it indicates the compatibility class but not the version, configuration, or privilege model.

\myparagraph{Software constraints}

A software constraint is induced when a \texttt{malware} or \texttt{tool} object is connected via \texttt{uses} relationships to a technique or campaign. Software constraints imply the presence of an installable artifact in the SUT\@. Their specificity depends on whether the object carries a version identifier or CPE reference; absent these fields, the constraint is satisfiable only at the family level.

\myparagraph{Vulnerability constraints}

A vulnerability constraint is induced when a CVE identifier appears in object descriptions or when a \texttt{vulnerability} object is connected via relationships to a campaign, technique, or software artifact. Vulnerability constraints are the most specific: they identify a product, affected version range, and attack surface. They are also the most actionable for SUT construction: deploying the vulnerable product at the specified version directly satisfies the constraint.

\subsection{SUT Requirement Profile}

For each campaign (and analogously for each intrusion set), we define a SUT requirement profile as the union of three components: (i) platform constraints induced by associated techniques, (ii) software constraints induced by linked malware/tool objects, and (iii) vulnerability constraints induced by CVE evidence. This profile is used only as an analysis artifact for coverage and specificity comparisons; it is not presented as an execution plan. For operational interpretation, we report campaign profiles in three evidence levels: coarse (software+platform), anchored (coarse plus version/CPE or campaign-level CVE), and exploit-pinned (coarse plus campaign-level CVE).

\subsection{SUT Compatibility Classes}

We classify each technique by the minimum virtualization backend required to satisfy its constraints:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Container-feasible (CF):} user-space artifacts with no kernel module, firmware, or Active Directory dependency.
\item \textbf{VM-required (VMR):} techniques involving kernel interactions, process injection, or boot-level persistence.
\item \textbf{Infrastructure-dependent (ID):} behaviors presupposing multi-host topology, domain controllers, or enterprise identity services.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Measurement Metrics}

\myparagraph{Platform coverage}

Fraction of techniques with at least one entry in \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}.

\myparagraph{Software reference rate}

Fraction of campaigns and intrusion sets linked to at least one \texttt{malware} or \texttt{tool} object.

\myparagraph{Vulnerability reference rate}

Fraction of campaigns and intrusion sets with at least one CVE linkage (direct or via associated software).

\myparagraph{SUT profile specificity}

Fraction of intrusion sets whose SUT profiles are unique under Jaccard distance threshold $\delta = 0.1$ when compared to all other intrusion set profiles.


% ===============================================================
\section{Dataset}\label{sec:dataset}

\subsection{Primary Dataset}

Following the measurement model in Section~\ref{sec:definitions}, we select datasets that expose the same core STIX structures while covering different ATT\&CK scopes. We use the \mitre{} \attack{} Enterprise bundle v18.1 as the primary source for all quantitative analyses. This bundle is the most extensively documented domain.

Table~\ref{tab:dataset} summarizes object counts across all analyzed sources.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{STIX object totals across ATT\&CK domains and sources. Active counts after deprecation filtering are reported in Section~\ref{sec:methodology}.}\label{tab:dataset}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Source} &
\textbf{Techniques} &
\textbf{Camp.} &
\textbf{IS} &
\textbf{Malware} &
\textbf{Tools} \\
\midrule
Enterprise & 835 & 52  & 187 & 696 & 91 \\
Mobile     & 190 & 3   & 17  & 121 & 2  \\
ICS        & 95  & 8   & 16  & 30  & 0  \\
CAPEC      & 615 & 0   & 0   & 0   & 0 \\
FiGHT      & 707 & 0   & 136 & 475 & 73 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The quantitative pipeline in this artifact traverses all five local bundles for cross-corpus coverage metrics. RQ2 and RQ3 remain focused on Enterprise because only Enterprise contains campaign coverage sufficient for the deployment-focused analyses. CAPEC is included as STIX-encoded attack-pattern knowledge for coverage contrast only; in this setup, it is not a campaign-level CTI corpus. Table~\ref{tab:dataset} reports raw object totals; all RQ metrics use active-object counts after deprecation/revocation filtering.

The Enterprise bundle contains 20,048 relationship objects. After deprecation and revocation filtering, our analysis covers 691 active techniques, 52 campaigns, 172 intrusion sets, and 784 software artifacts (693 malware~$+$~91 tools). The same bundle version and object-filtering conventions used here are shared with a prior procedural-gap study~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}.

\subsection{Extracted Fields}

For each \texttt{attack-pattern} object, we extract these fields:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.2em,itemsep=0.15em,topsep=0.15em]
\item \nolinkurl{x_mitre_platforms}
\item \nolinkurl{x_mitre_system_requirements}
\item \nolinkurl{x_mitre_permissions_required}
\item \nolinkurl{x_mitre_defense_bypassed}
\end{itemize}

For \texttt{malware} and \texttt{tool} objects, we extract names, aliases, platform tags, and any CPE or version strings in \nolinkurl{external_references}. CVE identifiers are extracted from \nolinkurl{description} fields and from linked \texttt{vulnerability} objects. Section~\ref{sec:methodology} turns these extracted fields into explicit measurement rules.


% ===============================================================
\section{Measurement Methodology}\label{sec:methodology}

\subsection{Extraction Pipeline}

We parse five locally available \stix{} bundles as JSON and normalize object identifiers. Enterprise, Mobile, and ICS drive the core analyses; CAPEC and FiGHT support cross-corpus coverage in RQ1. We exclude deprecated and revoked objects, which removes 144 of the 835 Enterprise attack-pattern objects, leaving 691 active techniques. We keep the same bundle version, object-filtering rules, and relationship traversal strategy as in~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}. Relationships are indexed by (\textit{source\_ref}, \textit{relationship\_type}, \textit{target\_ref}) triples to enable bidirectional traversal. For each campaign, the technique set is collected from all \texttt{attack-pattern} objects reachable via \texttt{uses} relationships; software and CVE associations are derived analogously.

For campaign-level target-platform inference, we use a conservative rule: platforms are inferred only from software-linked platform tags. Technique-level platforms are excluded from this step because they encode where a technique can run in general, not what a specific campaign targeted. Campaigns with no linked software are reported as \emph{platform unknown} instead of receiving inferred values. This avoids overstating environmental evidence when the bundle lacks software-level signals.

\subsection{SUT Compatibility Classification}

Each technique is assigned a compatibility class in order of precedence:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.2em,itemsep=0.2em,topsep=0.2em]
\item Classify as \textbf{CF} when the \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms} field is restricted to \texttt{Containers} or \texttt{Linux}, and the technique is not in kernel-interaction or boot-persistence clusters.

\item Classify as \textbf{VMR} when permissions-required fields indicate administrator privileges (Administrator, SYSTEM, or root). Also classified as \textbf{VMR} for privilege escalation and boot/firmware defense-evasion techniques.

\item If platform tags reference \texttt{Windows Domain} or \texttt{Identity Provider}, or the technique is Lateral Movement with an Active Directory software dependency: \textbf{ID}.

\item Default: \textbf{VMR}.
\end{enumerate}

This taxonomy is a conservative deployability proxy derived from structured fields. It estimates infrastructure requirements for replay planning and does not claim execution ground truth. Explicit rules cover \compatibilityexplicitassignmentpercentage\% of techniques; the remaining \compatibilityfallbackassignmentpercentage\% default to VMR under the conservative assumption that unresolved techniques require at least VM-level isolation. This fallback is intentionally conservative: it over-estimates rather than under-estimates infrastructure cost, which is the safer direction for cyber-range planning. We report default-sensitivity and explicit-rule coverage to separate rule-driven assignments from fallback-driven ones.

For independent auditing, the release package includes a stratified manual-validation packet of \compatibilityvalidationsamplesize{} techniques (\nolinkurl{results/audit/compatibility_validation_sample.csv}), including ATT\&CK IDs, URLs, predicted class, and firing-rule traces. Agreement summaries are produced by a separate evaluator script in the artifact package, so reviewers can recompute inter-rater agreement once adjudication labels are filled. The headline RQ2 percentages reported in this paper are computed using deterministic rules only; manual adjudication serves as external validation and does not feed into the reported metrics.

\subsection{Operational Pipeline and Ablation Framework}

To demonstrate the practical impact of the measured gaps, we implement an end-to-end SUT generation pipeline that progressively degrades input quality to quantify how each gap affects deployability. The pipeline operates in four stages:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.2em,itemsep=0.2em,topsep=0.2em]
\item \textbf{Structured-only}: Uses only STIX fields as currently encoded in ATT\&CK bundles.
\item \textbf{Structured + regex}: Adds regex-based version and CVE extraction from description fields.
\item \textbf{Structured + NLP}: Applies state-of-the-art CTI extraction models to unstructured text.
\item \textbf{Structured + external}: Incorporates vulnerability databases and software repositories.
\end{enumerate}

Each stage outputs a deployability score (0--1) representing the fraction of techniques that can be instantiated with sufficient environment detail. The pipeline logs failure modes by technique class, constraint type, and evidence source, enabling precise attribution of deployability losses to specific CTI gaps.

\paragraph{Failure mode taxonomy}

We categorize SUT generation failures into four classes:

\begin{description}
\item[Platform ambiguity] Technique lists multiple platforms without specifying which was targeted in the incident.
\item[Software version missing] Software object present but no version/CPE to pin installation.
\item[CVE fragmentation] Vulnerability mentioned only in free text, requiring extraction validation.
\item[Dependency chain incomplete] Prerequisite software or service not specified in CTI.
\end{description}

The pipeline measures the contribution of each failure class to overall deployability loss, providing actionable guidance for CTI standards improvement.

\paragraph{Baseline automation comparison}

To bound the automation potential, we compare our structured-only pipeline against two baselines:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Rule-based planner}: Uses deterministic mapping from ATT\&CK fields to IaC templates.
\item \textbf{LLM-assisted}: GPT-4 constrained by STIX metadata to generate environment specifications.
\end{itemize}

Both baselines operate under the same input constraints as our structured-only stage, ensuring fair comparison. The LLM baseline receives technique descriptions, platform hints, and linked software objects, but no external knowledge beyond its training cutoff.

\subsection{Profile Specificity Analysis}

For each intrusion set $g$ we encode its SUT profile as a binary vector over all software and CVE identifiers in the corpus. Pairwise Jaccard distances are computed and the fraction of groups with at least one neighbor within $\delta = 0.1$ is reported as the SUT profile confusion rate. Section~\ref{sec:analysis} reports the measured values under these rules.

\myparagraph{Artifact package (double-blind placeholder)}

The reproducibility package used in this study contains the measurement code, generated datasets, audit CSVs, and release checks. The artifact includes a one-command verifier (\texttt{measurement/sut/release\_check.sh}) that reruns extraction, rebuilds figures, regenerates traceability tables, and compiles the manuscript. The anonymized artifact link is supplied in the submission materials and will be replaced by the public repository URL in the camera-ready version.

\section{Analysis}\label{sec:analysis}

% Keep one decimal place for percentages, except when value < 1.0%.

We report results in RQ order: coverage (RQ1), deployability and compatibility (RQ2), and then profile specificity (RQ3).

\subsection{Platform Constraint Coverage (RQ1)}
% TASK (RQ1/platform):
% 1) Compute rho_P for measured corpora and report all five bundles.
% 2) Compute fill rate of x_mitre_system_requirements.
% 3) Confirm filters (deprecated/revoked) match Methods.

Of the \enterpriseplatformcount{} active Enterprise techniques, all \enterpriseplatformcount{} (\enterpriseplatformpct\%) carry at least one entry in \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms}, so platform annotation is effectively mandatory in current ATT\&CK curation. In contrast, \texttt{x\_mitre\_system\_requirements}, the most specific platform field, is populated in \enterprisesystemrequirementscount{} techniques (\enterprisesystemrequirementspct\%). Structured, fine-grained OS and software-version constraints are absent in the Enterprise environment.

Platform-field coverage is also high in Mobile (\mobileplatformpct\%), ICS (\icsplatformpercentage\%), and FiGHT (\fightplatformpercentage\%) under the same active-object filter. CAPEC does not use \texttt{x\_mitre\_platforms} in this dataset because it encodes abstract attack patterns rather than platform-bound behaviors.

Across corpora, the same pattern appears: platform-field coverage is high, software-link coverage is moderate, and CVE-linked coverage is low. CAPEC zeros are structural properties of the bundle (no \texttt{malware}/\texttt{tool} objects and no CVE mentions in active \texttt{attack-pattern} objects), not missing data caused by the measurement pipeline. Figure~\ref{fig:coverage_cross_corpus} summarizes this cross-corpus coverage pattern directly.

\begin{figure}[!tb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figures/coverage_template.tex}}
\caption{\label{fig:coverage_cross_corpus}Cross-corpus coverage of structured SUT-relevant signals at attack-pattern level, with per-corpus denominators shown as $n$ labels. Platform fields are near-universal, software-link coverage is moderate, and CVE-linked coverage is sparse across ATT\&CK and related corpora. CAPEC zeros are schema-driven in this dataset.}
\Description{Grouped bars comparing platform, software-link, and CVE-link coverage across Enterprise, Mobile, ICS, CAPEC, and FiGHT.}
\end{figure}

\myparagraph{Campaign-level platform signal under software-only inference}

Applying the software-only platform rule to all \enterpriseactivecampaigncount{} Enterprise campaigns yields a platform signal for \enterprisecampaignswithplatformsignalcount{} campaigns (\enterprisecampaignswithplatformsignalpct\%); the remaining \enterprisecampaignsplatformunknowncount{} campaigns (\enterprisecampaignsplatformunknownpct\%) are genuinely unknown under structured fields alone. These campaigns are \emph{FrostyGoop Incident}, \emph{KV Botnet Activity}, \emph{Leviathan Australian Intrusions}, \emph{SPACEHOP Activity}, and \emph{ShadowRay}.

Table~\ref{tab:campaign_os_families} summarizes the OS-family distribution among campaigns with signal. These values are inferred from software-linked platform tags in \attack{}, rather than from explicit campaign target declarations. They should be interpreted as evidence of compatibility. Campaigns may be multi-OS, so OS-family counts can exceed the number of campaigns with signal. When the platform signal is driven by a single cross-platform tool (e.g., Linux/Windows/macOS tags), the inferred set is a compatibility envelope rather than a confirmed target-OS declaration.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{Software-derived OS-family evidence across Enterprise campaigns. Values are multi-label inferences from software-linked platform tags and represent evidence of compatibility, not confirmed target-OS attribution. \% signal uses $n=\enterprisecampaignswithplatformsignalcount{}$; \% total uses $n=\enterpriseactivecampaigncount{}$.}\label{tab:campaign_os_families}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{lrrr}
\toprule
\textbf{OS family} & \textbf{Campaigns} & \textbf{\% signal} & \textbf{\% total} \\
\midrule
Windows & \campaignoswindowscount{} & \campaignoswindowspctsignal\% & \campaignoswindowspcttotal\% \\
Linux   & \campaignoslinuxcount{} & \campaignoslinuxpctsignal\% & \campaignoslinuxpcttotal\% \\
macOS   & \campaignosmacoscount{} & \campaignosmacospctsignal\% & \campaignosmacospcttotal\% \\
\midrule
\textit{Platform unknown} & \enterprisecampaignsplatformunknowncount{} & --- & \enterprisecampaignsplatformunknownpct\% \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Accordingly, OS-family evidence should not be used as a standalone attribution signal; in this paper, it is used only as environmental compatibility evidence for SUT construction. With platform coverage established, we next test whether software references provide enough precision to instantiate reproducible targets.

\subsection{Software Reference Rate (RQ1, RQ2)}
% TASK (RQ1/RQ2 software):
% 1) Measure software-link rates for campaigns and intrusion sets.
% 2) Detect version signal from name/alias/external_references.
% 3) Count software objects that include CPE.

The Enterprise bundle contains \enterpriseactivemalwarecount{} active malware and \enterpriseactivetoolcount{} tool objects (\enterpriseactivesoftwarecount{} total). Of the \enterpriseactivecampaigncount{} campaigns, \enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarecount{} (\enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarepercentage\%) are linked to at least one software artifact (95\% confidence interval: \enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarecilow{}\% to \enterprisecampaignswithsoftwarecihigh{}\%). Among the \enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} active intrusion sets, \enterpriseintrusionsetswithsoftwarecount{} (\enterpriseintrusionsetswithsoftwarepercentage\%) carry at least one software association.

Specificity is the limiting factor. Of all \enterpriseactivesoftwarecount{} software objects, only \softwarewithversionsignalpercentage\% contain any version indicator in \texttt{name}, \texttt{aliases}, or \texttt{external\_references} (95\% confidence interval: \softwarewithversionsignalcilow{}\% to \softwarewithversionsignalcihigh{}\%). CPE identifiers, which directly support version-pinned installation, appear in \softwarewithcpepercentage\% of software objects. Software \emph{presence} is well documented, but software \emph{specificity} for automated SUT construction is largely absent. The distribution of specificity levels is summarized in Figure~\ref{fig:software_specificity}. The key reading is that almost all software references are family-level labels without a version pin.

\begin{figure}[!tb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figures/software_specificity_template.tex}}
% FIGURE NOTE:
% - Stacked bar must sum to 100%.
% - Include total N in caption or legend.
\caption{Most Enterprise software evidence is not version-pinned (N=\enterpriseactivesoftwarecount{} active software objects), limiting direct SUT instantiation.}\label{fig:software_specificity}
\Description{Stacked bar with three segments over active Enterprise software objects: no version and no CPE, textual version without CPE, and CPE present.}
\end{figure}

At the campaign level, this software-specificity bottleneck propagates into profile completeness: coarse profiles are common, while anchored and exploit-pinned profiles are rare. Figure~\ref{fig:tier_collapse} shows this collapse directly.

\begin{figure}[!tb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figures/tier_collapse_template.tex}}
\caption{\label{fig:tier_collapse}Campaign SUT profile completeness collapses from coarse to anchored/exploit-pinned levels in Enterprise ($N=\enterpriseactivecampaigncount{}$ campaigns). Linkage abundance does not imply version-pinned SUT precision.}
\Description{Vertical bar chart showing campaign percentages for coarse, anchored, and exploit-pinned profile tiers, with a sharp drop from coarse to the higher-evidence tiers.}
\end{figure}

\finding{1}{Coverage is high at coarse granularity: \campaignsuttiercoarsepct\% of campaigns have a coarse SUT profile, but only \campaignsuttieranchoredpct\% reach version-anchored precision. The bottleneck is specificity, not linkage.}

This result also has a positive operational interpretation: structured fields already support the automatic generation of coarse environment profiles for \campaignsuttiercoarsecount{} campaigns, which are useful for initial environment narrowing before analyst refinement.

Given this software-specificity bottleneck, we next measure how often vulnerability evidence can provide exploit-level anchors.

\subsection{Vulnerability Reference Rate (RQ1, RQ2)}
% TASK (RQ1/RQ2 CVE):
% 1) Extract CVEs from structured fields and free text with one parser.
% 2) Deduplicate CVE IDs before counting.
% 3) Report campaign/group coverage and structured vs text split.

CVE identifiers extracted from the Enterprise bundle total \cveuniquecount{} unique CVEs. The bundle contains zero dedicated vulnerability SDOs, so CVE evidence appears in structured external references or free-text descriptions. Of the \cveuniquecount{} CVEs, \cvetechniqueonlycount{} appear only as illustrative examples in technique descriptions (for example, \emph{Exploit Public-Facing Application} citing CVE-2016-6662). These are not campaign-specific. The remaining \cveactionablecount{} are actionable because they are linked to software, campaign, or intrusion-set objects.

Coverage remains low at the campaign and group levels. Only \entcampaignswithcvecount{} campaigns (\entcampaignswithcvepct\%, 95\% confidence interval: \entcampaignswithcvecilow{}\% to \entcampaignswithcvecihigh{}\%) carry any CVE reference, and only \entintrusionsetswithcvecount{} intrusion sets (\entintrusionsetswithcvepct\%) have at least one CVE, which is lower than the 31.6\% ATT\&CK-group CVE coverage reported by Saha et al., who used full threat-report parsing rather than structured STIX fields alone~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}. This comparison indicates a gap between reported intelligence and encoded intelligence. More importantly, vulnerability evidence is fragmented across representation layers: some CVE identifiers appear in structured external references, while many others appear only in free-text descriptions. This separation limits the operational utility of vulnerability evidence, even when it is present in the corpus.

To test whether this gap is actionable, we perform an in-corpus enrichment step that promotes CVE mentions from free-text descriptions into machine-readable CVE slots, then count object-level coverage. Under this normalization, campaign-level CVE coverage rises from \entcampaignswithcvestructuredcount{}/\enterpriseactivecampaigncount{} (\entcampaignswithcvestructuredpct\%) to \entcampaignswithcvecount{}/\enterpriseactivecampaigncount{} (\entcampaignswithcvepct\%), a gain of \entcampaignswithcveenrichmentgaincount{} campaigns (+\entcampaignswithcveenrichmentgainpp{} percentage points). For intrusion sets, coverage rises from \entintrusionsetswithcvestructuredcount{}/\enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} (\entintrusionsetswithcvestructuredpct\%) to \entintrusionsetswithcvecount{}/\enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} (\entintrusionsetswithcvepct\%), a gain of \entintrusionsetswithcveenrichmentgaincount{} (+\entintrusionsetswithcveenrichmentgainpp{} percentage points). Figure~\ref{fig:cve_location} summarizes the operational attrition path from all detected CVEs to campaign-level usable evidence.

This enrichment experiment isolates the practical headroom of text-based extraction within the current corpus. The gain remains modest because most campaigns do not provide explicit evidence of vulnerability in any form. Structured CTI, therefore, lacks both explicit CVE encoding and consistent textual reporting of exploitable vulnerabilities.

\myparagraph{Campaign-linked exploited CVEs}
Among the \cveactionablecount{} actionable CVEs, \campaignlinkedcvecount{} are explicitly tied to campaign objects in the Enterprise STIX dataset. Table~\ref{tab:campaign_linked_cves} lists these CVEs and the five campaigns they belong to; they represent the strongest structured vulnerability evidence for SUT instantiation in the current bundle.

Figure~\ref{fig:compatibility_distribution} combines class distribution with a non-CF sensitivity interval, including fallback floor and ceiling and the rule-only resolved estimate, so the chart adds uncertainty context beyond Table~\ref{tab:compat}.

\begin{figure}[!tb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figures/compatibility_distribution_template.tex}}
\caption{\label{fig:compatibility_distribution}Compatibility-class distribution for active Enterprise techniques. Most techniques are VM-required (VMR) or infrastructure-dependent (ID), bounding the coverage of container-only emulation setups. The right-side interval summarizes non-CF sensitivity under fallback choices, and the marker reports the rule-only resolved estimate.}
\Description{Bar chart of compatibility-class percentages and counts for CF, VMR, and ID across active Enterprise techniques, plus a non-CF sensitivity interval with floor, ceiling, and rule-only resolved marker.}
\end{figure}

Techniques classified as VMR or ID cannot be reproduced faithfully in a container-only environment. Campaigns involving Lateral Movement, boot-level persistence, or Credential Access through domain services consistently fall into VMR or ID classes, limiting the applicability of container-based cyber ranges for full-campaign emulation. Operationally, this result places a structural bound on container-only cyber ranges: a large fraction of ATT\&CK techniques cannot be evaluated without VM or multi-host infrastructure.

The conservative fallback materially affects absolute rates. When unresolved techniques are forced to CF, VMR, or ID, the non-CF share spans \compatibilitynoncffloorpercentage\% to \compatibilitynoncfceilingpercentage\%. Without fallback assignment, explicit rules cover \compatibilityrulecoveragepercentage\% of techniques, and the resolved subset is \compatibilitynoncfresolvedpercentage\% in the non-CF class. We therefore interpret \compatibilitynoncfceilingpercentage\% as an upper-bound planning signal, not calibrated ground truth.

\finding{2}{Under conservative fallback, \compatibilitynoncfceilingpercentage\% of techniques are in the non-CF class. Under rule-only coverage, \compatibilitynoncfresolvedpercentage\% of resolved techniques remain in the non-CF class. VM and infrastructure requirements remain dominant after sensitivity checks.}

All \enterpriseplatformcount{} techniques receive a deterministic class assignment (CF, VMR, ID). This supports automated backend selection even when faithful replay is not supported. Appendix Figure~\ref{fig:compatibility_by_tactic} decomposes this result by tactic and shows that non-CF dominance is broad across the Enterprise tactic spectrum, not concentrated in a single tactic family.

\begin{figure}[!htb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figures/compatibility_by_tactic_template.tex}}
\caption{\label{fig:compatibility_by_tactic}Exploratory tactic-level view of non-CF share
for Enterprise techniques. Each row shows the non-container-feasible fraction
(VMR+ID) within one tactic, with support $n$ shown per row.}
\Description{Horizontal bars showing non-CF percentage by ATT and CK tactic, with per-tactic support counts.}
\end{figure}

Having established that most techniques require VM-level or higher infrastructure (RQ2), we now ask whether the observable SUT properties can distinguish between threat actors (RQ3).

\subsection{SUT Profile Specificity (RQ3)}
% TASK (RQ3 specificity):
% 1) Build profiles in two settings: software-only and software+CVE.
% 2) Compute nearest-neighbor Jaccard distance and confusion at delta=0.1.
% 3) Recompute unique-profile percentages from the same matrix.

Of the \enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} active intrusion sets, \sutprofileuniquesoftwarepercentage\% have a unique SUT profile at Jaccard distance $\delta = 0.1$ when profiles use software constraints only. This value remains \sutprofileuniquesoftwarecvepercentage\% when CVE constraints are added. That result is consistent with sparse CVE linkage: only \entintrusionsetswithcvecount{} intrusion sets carry any CVE evidence. The remaining \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecvepercentage\% of groups still share their SUT profile with at least one other group.

Operationally, a SUT built for group $g$ can still match profiles of other groups within distance $\delta$. This limits claims that a successful emulation is specific to one adversary. The same ambiguity pattern is observed in behavioral attribution studies~\cite{DBLP:conf/uss/SahaMBCVL25}; here, it manifests at the environmental level. A bootstrap stability check with 5{,}000 replicates at $\delta = 0.10$ yields confusion \bootstrapconfusionpct\% (confidence interval: \bootstrapconfusioncilow{}\%--\bootstrapconfusioncihigh{}\%) and unique-profile rate \bootstrapuniquepct\% (confidence interval: \bootstrapuniquecilow{}\%--\bootstrapuniquecihigh{}\%). Table~\ref{tab:ablation_rq3} summarizes ablation settings at $\delta = 0.10$.

The near-uniform confusion across feature combinations indicates that, in this corpus, the additional structured signals, including CVE, platform, and OS family, do not materially improve profile separability beyond that provided by software identity alone. This is itself a finding: it bounds the expected gain from augmenting profiles with currently available structured fields. Compatibility-summary features yield a modest 0.6~pp improvement. A cardinality-preserving null model (\nullmodeliterations{} iterations, randomizing software identities while keeping per-IS counts) yields mean confusion \nullmodelconfusionmeanpct\% (p05=\nullmodelconfusionplowpct\%, p95=\nullmodelconfusionphighpct\%; permutation $p$=\nullmodelpvalue{}). The observed \nullmodelobservedconfusionpct\% falls within this interval, indicating that most measured profile overlap is explained by evidence cardinality rather than by distinctive environmental constraints encoded in CTI. The practical implication remains the same: minimum-evidence thresholds ($k \geq 2$) are necessary regardless of the mechanism.

\finding{3}{Profile confusion is concentrated at low evidence levels: it drops to 0\% at $k \geq 2$ linked software items (\(n=\thresholdktwosample\)) and remains 0\% at $k \geq 3$ (\(n=\thresholdkthreesample\)).}

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{RQ3 feature ablation at $\delta=0.10$ (n = \enterpriseactiveintrusionsetcount{} intrusion sets).}\label{tab:ablation_rq3}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{3.15cm}rr@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Profile Setting} & \textbf{Unique (\%)} & \textbf{Confused (\%)} \\
\midrule
Software only & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarepercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarepercentage\% \\
Software + CVE & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarecvepercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecvepercentage\% \\
Software + platform & \sutprofileuniquesoftwareplatformpercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwareplatformpercentage\% \\
Software + CVE + platform & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarecveplatformpercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecveplatformpercentage\% \\
Software + OS family & \sutprofileuniquesoftwarefamilyonlypercentage\% & \sutprofileconfusionsoftwarefamilyonlypercentage\% \\
\textbf{Software + compatibility} & \textbf{\sutprofileuniquesoftwarecompatpercentage\%} & \textbf{\sutprofileconfusionsoftwarecompatpercentage\%} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure}[!tb]
\centering
\resizebox{\columnwidth}{!}{\input{figures/ablation_template.tex}}
\caption{Profile ambiguity collapses by $k \geq 2$ linked software items in software-only profiles ($\delta=0.10$) and remains collapsed for higher thresholds.}\label{fig:ablation_rq3}
\Description{Vertical bar chart of confusion percentage for all intrusion sets and thresholded subsets k>=1, k>=2, and k>=3, with per-subset sample sizes.}
\end{figure}

The full nearest-neighbor CDF comparison is provided in Appendix~\ref{sec:appendix_supp} (Figure~\ref{fig:jaccard_cdf}) and confirms the same reading: adding CVE evidence does not materially shift separability at $\delta = 0.10$.

\subsection{Operational Pipeline Results}

We now present the end-to-end pipeline results that demonstrate the practical impact of the measured gaps on SUT generation deployability.

\paragraph{Deployability scores across pipeline stages}

Table~\ref{tab:pipeline_results} shows the deployability scores for each pipeline stage across the Enterprise technique corpus. The structured-only stage achieves only \pipelinesructuredscore\% deployability, confirming that current CTI encoding is insufficient for automated SUT generation. Adding regex-based extraction improves this to \pipelineregexscore\% (+\pipelineregexgainpp~pp), while state-of-the-art NLP extraction reaches \pipelinenlpscore\% (+\pipelinenlpgainpp~pp over structured-only). Incorporating external vulnerability databases achieves the highest score at \pipelineexternalscore\% (+\pipelineexternalgainpp~pp).

\begin{table}[!htb]
\caption{SUT generation deployability across pipeline stages (n = \enterpriseplatformcount{} techniques).}\label{tab:pipeline_results}
\centering\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}>{\raggedright\arraybackslash}p{3.2cm}rr@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Pipeline Stage} & \textbf{Deployability (\%)} & \textbf{Gain vs. Structured (pp)} \\
\midrule
Structured-only & \pipelinesructuredscore\% & -- \\
Structured + regex & \pipelineregexscore\% & +\pipelineregexgainpp \\
Structured + NLP & \pipelinenlpscore\% & +\pipelinenlpgainpp \\
Structured + external & \pipelineexternalscore\% & +\pipelineexternalgainpp \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Failure mode analysis}

Figure~\ref{fig:failure_modes} decomposes deployability losses by failure class. Software version missing is the dominant failure mode, accounting for \failureversionpercentage\% of all failures across all stages. Platform ambiguity contributes \failureplatformpercentage\%, while CVE fragmentation and dependency chain incomplete account for \failurecvepercentage\% and \failuredependencypercentage\% respectively.

\paragraph{Baseline automation comparison}

Table~\ref{tab:baseline_comparison} compares our structured-only pipeline against automation baselines. The rule-based planner achieves slightly higher deployability (\baserulescore\%) than our structured-only approach by leveraging deterministic heuristics for platform selection. The LLM-assisted baseline reaches \basellmscore\%, demonstrating that current language models can partially compensate for missing structured fields but still fall short of full automation.

\finding{4}{Even with state-of-the-art NLP extraction, deployability caps at \pipelinenlpscore\%, indicating that approximately \pipelinegappercentage\% of SUT requirements remain unattainable from current CTI representations alone.}

These operational results demonstrate that the measured gaps have concrete consequences for automated SUT generation. The pipeline provides a reproducible framework for tracking progress as CTI standards evolve and for evaluating the marginal value of enrichment techniques.

% ===============================================================
\section{Discussion}\label{sec:discussion}

\subsection{Implications for Reproducible APT Emulation}

Our results show that structured CTI supports only part of the information required for SUT construction. Platform constraints are common but coarse, software constraints identify artifacts but rarely specify versions, and CVE constraints are sparse and split between structured and free-text fields. Automated SUT generation from CTI fields alone is therefore not feasible in this dataset. A minimum viable workflow still needs one analyst-driven enrichment step to pin software versions and map platform hints to concrete OS images.

The operational pipeline results quantify this gap precisely: structured-only CTI achieves only \pipelinesructuredscore\% deployability, meaning that approximately \pipelinegappercentage\% of SUT requirements cannot be satisfied without external enrichment. Even state-of-the-art NLP extraction caps at \pipelinenlpscore\%, leaving a substantial automation gap that cannot be closed by text processing alone.

This result still supports partial automation. Three operations are already derivable from structured fields: coarse environment narrowing from platform and software links, deterministic backend selection via CF/VMR/ID classes, and evidence-filtered profile screening ($k \geq 2$). Campaigns with only coarse evidence should be treated as compatibility guidance rather than replay-ready specifications; container-only ranges are bounded by \compatibilitycontainerfeasiblepercentage\% CF coverage; and profiles with fewer than two linked software items carry elevated confusion risk.

\paragraph{Cost-benefit implications for cyber range design}

The failure mode analysis reveals that software version missing accounts for \failureversionpercentage\% of all deployability losses across pipeline stages. This suggests that CTI curation efforts should prioritize version information collection as the highest-impact intervention. Platform ambiguity, while less frequent (\failureplatformpercentage\%), requires different remediation strategies such as incident-specific targeting metadata.

The baseline comparison shows that LLM-assisted approaches can achieve \basellmscore\% deployability—substantially better than structured-only but still requiring significant human oversight. Rule-based planners perform slightly better (\baserulescore\%) by leveraging deterministic heuristics, suggesting that hybrid approaches may offer the best cost-benefit ratio for practitioners.

\subsection{Container vs.\ VM Trade-offs}

The compatibility classification shows that \compatibilityvmrequiredpercentage\% (VMR) plus \compatibilityinfrastructuredependentpercentage\% (ID) of Enterprise techniques require VM or full-infrastructure backends. Research groups designing cyber ranges exclusively around container orchestration will systematically exclude entire tactic clusters, in particular, boot-level persistence, kernel injection, and enterprise identity attacks. VM-based environments cover a substantially larger fraction of the technique space at the cost of greater infrastructure complexity.

This conclusion should be read with the sensitivity bounds in Section~\ref{sec:analysis}: non-CF dominance remains, but exact percentages depend on the fallback policy for unresolved techniques.

\subsection{SUT Susceptibility and Attribution}

The SUT profile confusion analysis shows that environment-level profiles do not uniquely identify a single threat actor. A defender who builds a SUT matching the CVE and software profile of a particular APT group is also building a SUT susceptible to many other groups. Claims of ``APT-X-specific emulation'' should therefore be qualified by reporting the SUT confusion rate at a stated distance threshold.

For attribution-oriented interpretations, a minimum-evidence threshold should be made explicit before actor-level conclusions. This paper does not set a universal threshold; it shows that sparse SUT profiles increase overlap and that threshold selection must be calibrated using measured confusion behavior. Under the software-only profile setting, confusion is \thresholdkoneconfusionpct\% at $k \geq 1$, drops to \thresholdktwoconfusionpct\% for profiles with at least two software links ($n=\thresholdktwosample$), remains \thresholdkthreeconfusionpct\% at $k \geq 3$ ($n=\thresholdkthreesample$), and reaches \thresholdkfiveconfusionpct\% for profiles with at least five links ($n=\thresholdkfivesample$). The delta sensitivity check follows the same direction: \deltazerozerofiveconfusionpct\% at $\delta=0.05$, \deltazerotenconfusionpct\% at $\delta=0.10$, and \deltazerofifteenconfusionpct\% at $\delta=0.15$.

\subsection{Path Toward Machine-Actionable SUT Specifications}

Our measurements identify three concrete gaps.

\myparagraph{Gap 1: software presence without version}
Most software objects linked to campaigns lack version information and CPE identifiers. A \texttt{malware} object, such as \emph{Cobalt Strike}, does not specify which version should be deployed or which CVEs are in scope. A direct schema extension is to add optional \texttt{x\_mitre\_version\_range} and \texttt{cpe23uri} fields to \stix{} \texttt{malware} and \texttt{tool} objects.

\myparagraph{Gap 2: CVE evidence fragmented across fields}
In the Enterprise corpus, \cvefreetextonlycount{} of \cveuniquecount{} CVEs (\cvefromfreetextpct\%) appear only in natural-language \texttt{description} fields, and there are zero \texttt{vulnerability} SDOs. The remaining evidence appears in structured \texttt{external\_references} entries. As a result, automated extraction depends on text processing outside the structured representation. A direct improvement is to promote CVE mentions into structured fields during curation.

Recent work points in the same direction: Meng et al.~\cite{meng2025uncoveringvulnerabilitiesllmassistedcti} show that LLMs applied to CTI exhibit \emph{environmental unawareness} in 7.8--14.3\% of cases, which is consistent with the gap measured here. We do not evaluate this remediation in this paper.

\myparagraph{Enrichment headroom}
To quantify the practical ceiling of text-based enrichment, we apply the same version-detection regex to software \texttt{description} fields that currently lack a structured version signal. This recovers \softwareversionenrichmentgaincount{} additional objects (+\softwareversionenrichmentgainpp{}~pp), lifting the version signal from \softwarewithversionsignalpercentage\% to \softwareversionenrichedtotalpct\%. For CVEs, scanning free-text fields adds \entcampaignswithcveenrichmentgaincount{} campaigns (+\entcampaignswithcveenrichmentgainpp{}~pp), raising campaign CVE coverage from \entcampaignswithcvestructuredpct\% to \entcampaignswithcvepct\%. These gains are modest in absolute terms but represent the entire marginal value of free-text extraction for existing objects.

\myparagraph{Gap 3: no SUT compatibility signal}
The \attack{} schema carries no field indicating whether a technique requires a container, a VM, or a full enterprise topology. Our CF/VMR/ID classification is heuristic and inherits the incompleteness of the underlying fields. A direct extension would be a standardized \texttt{x\_mitre\_sut\_class} field.

\subsection{Threats to Validity}

Construct validity is limited because our measurements capture only what is explicitly encoded in STIX fields; version and configuration information present in source reports but absent from structured fields is not captured. Internal validity is constrained by the asynchronous update pipeline between the ATT\&CK website and downloadable STIX bundles, which may cause our extracted counts to underestimate true association rates; the same asynchrony was observed in the companion study~\cite{ferraz2026proceduralsemanticsgapstructured}. External validity is bounded by the focus on public ATT\&CK datasets; commercial CTI feeds may carry richer software version data.

Campaign-level platform inference also uses software-linked platform tags as a proxy for compatibility. Multi-platform tools can inflate OS-family counts and do not prove the exact operating system targeted in a specific incident.

Finally, because this study and its companion analysis share the same data baseline and extraction conventions, any upstream corpus bias propagates to both axes, thereby strengthening comparability but also coupling sensitivity to ATT\&CK curation artifacts.


% ===============================================================
\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}

We presented a SUT-centered measurement of \mitre{} \attack{} structured CTI and quantified the environment evidence required for reproducible adversary emulation. Across five corpora (Enterprise, Mobile, ICS, CAPEC, and FiGHT), platforms are named but rarely version-qualified, software artifacts appear without actionable installation metadata (\softwarenoversionnocpepercentage\%), and vulnerability evidence appears in fewer than 1 in 10 campaigns.

The results define the current automation boundary. Our operational pipeline demonstrates that structured-only CTI achieves only \pipelinesructuredscore\% deployability for automated SUT generation, leaving \pipelinegappercentage\% of requirements unattainable without external enrichment. Even state-of-the-art NLP extraction caps at \pipelinenlpscore\%, confirming that the measured gaps represent fundamental limitations of current CTI encoding rather than extraction challenges.

These findings directly answer the research questions: coverage remains incomplete for replay-ready SUT derivation (RQ1), container-only ranges cover only a limited fraction of techniques (RQ2), and profile specificity depends on the evidence cardinality (RQ3). The failure mode analysis identifies software version missing as the dominant bottleneck (\failureversionpercentage\% of losses), providing clear guidance for CTI standards improvement.

More broadly, the results show that the current CTI ecosystem encodes adversary behavior with far greater fidelity than execution environment constraints. Until environment semantics are incorporated into structured CTI representations, fully automated adversary-emulation pipelines will remain infeasible. The pipeline framework introduced in this paper provides a reproducible baseline for measuring progress as CTI standards evolve.

The companion study showed that structured CTI does not encode \emph{how} to execute procedures. This paper shows that it also under-specifies \emph{where} to execute them. Together, the two studies define the procedural and environmental limits of structured CTI for machine-actionable emulation and establish concrete targets for standards improvement.
